description: Add requiresHuman field to PRD tasks. When a requiresHuman task completes, the loop auto-pauses for interactive human input, injects feedback as session guidance, and runs a Claude call to mutate downstream tasks based on the feedback. Works in both single-loop and batch mode (overrides yes_mode). Optional per-task timeout for auto-continue.

criteria: Rust: No warnings in `cargo check` output
Rust: No warnings in `cargo clippy` output
Rust: All tests pass with `cargo test`
No breaking changes to existing APIs unless explicitly required
globalAcceptanceCriteria.description: These criteria apply to ALL implementation tasks

mergeStrategy: Merge to main after MILESTONE-FINAL passes. Squash commits optional.

priorityPhilosophy.description: The hierarchy of what matters most when implementing tasks
hierarchy: 1. PLAN — Approach before code. Anticipate edge cases. Consider 2-3 approaches, pick the best

2. PHASE 2 FOUNDATION — If a more sophisticated solution costs ~1 day now but saves ~2+ weeks post-launch, take that trade-off (1:10 ratio or better). We are pre-launch; foundations compound enormously
3. FUNCTIONING CODE — Pragmatic, reliable code, wired in according to the plan
4. CORRECTNESS — Self-critique after code. Code compiles, type-checks, passes all tests deterministically
5. CODE QUALITY — Quality dimensions explicit. Clean code, good patterns, no warnings
principles: Quality dimensions explicit — read qualityDimensions on every task; know what 'good' looks like before coding
Approach before code — consider 2-3 approaches with tradeoffs, pick the best, then implement
Phase 2 foundation — prefer solutions that lay strong post-launch foundations; if ~1 day now saves ~2+ weeks later (1:10+ ratio), take the sophisticated path
Edge cases = test cases — every known edge case must have a corresponding test; boundaries are where bugs hide
Self-critique after code — review your own implementation for correctness, style, and performance before moving on
Ship solid working code with tests to prove it
Handle Option/Result explicitly; avoid unwrap() in production code

prohibitedOutcomes: Tests that only assert 'no crash' or check type without verifying content
Tests that mirror implementation internals (break when refactoring)
Abstractions with only one concrete use
Error messages that don't identify what went wrong
Catch-all error handlers that swallow context

priorityGuidelines.medium: 21-50: Fix in current phase if time permits

userStories.description: Create handle_human_review() function that displays a context-rich banner (task ID, title, notes), reads multi-line stdin input (reusing handle_pause pattern), supports optional timeout, and records input as tagged session guidance.
userStories.notes: For timeout: spawn a reader thread that sends lines over mpsc::channel. Main thread uses recv_timeout. If timeout fires, collect whatever lines arrived. For testability, consider accepting a generic BufRead instead of hardcoding stdin — but this changes the signature from handle_pause. Pragmatic approach: keep stdin hardcoded for now (matching handle_pause), test banner output via captured stderr.

Timeout must collect partial input, not discard it
requiresHuman override of yes_mode happens at the CALLER (engine.rs), not in this function

acceptanceCriteria: Completed task IDs collected from all detection paths (completed_tags, git, output scan, external reconciliation)
For each completed task with requires_human=true, query task title and notes from DB
Call handle_human_review() with task context, regardless of yes_mode

userStories.description: In engine.rs, after all completion detection paths converge (~line 1811) and before iteration counter tracking (~line 1813), check each completed task for requires_human=true and call handle_human_review(). Override yes_mode for requiresHuman tasks. Skip review for pre-completed tasks.
userStories.notes: The key insight: requiresHuman overrides yes_mode at the ENGINE level (the caller), not inside handle_human_review. The function itself doesn't know about yes_mode. Engine code: `if task.requires_human { handle_human_review(...) }` — no yes_mode check wrapping it. For collecting completed task IDs: look at completed_tags Vec, the git detection branch, scan_output_for_completed_tasks return, and external reconciliation count. A practical approach: maintain a Vec<String> of completed_this_iteration IDs and append to it in each detection path.

correctness: Must fire after ALL completion paths (not just <completed> tags)
Must not fire for pre-completed tasks (passes: true at import)
requiresHuman must override yes_mode — this is the batch mode fix
performance: DB query for requires_human is a primary key lookup — negligible cost
Only query when a task actually completes (not every iteration)
style: Insert review check cleanly between existing completion and tracking blocks
Minimize diff size in the already-large engine.rs

userStories.title: Engine integration: trigger human review after task completion

acceptanceCriteria: Build mutation prompt: human feedback + remaining todo tasks from PRD JSON + instruction to update
Spawn Claude subprocess using existing run_claude pattern (sonnet model default)
Parse Claude output for updated PRD JSON (or individual task mutations)
Apply modifications atomically to PRD JSON (temp file + rename, following update_prd_task_passes pattern)
Re-sync DB by re-importing modified tasks (use existing init/import flow)
Keep backup of pre-mutation PRD JSON (.json.bak)
On any failure (spawn, parse, write): log error, continue with session guidance only
Log summary: 'Task mutation: N tasks modified, M added, K marked irrelevant'
userStories.description: After collecting human input for a review checkpoint, spawn a Claude call to process the feedback against remaining todo tasks. Claude modifies task descriptions, notes, acceptance criteria, priorities, or marks tasks irrelevant. Apply changes atomically to PRD JSON and re-sync DB. Graceful degradation to session-guidance-only on failure.
userStories.notes: This is the most complex task. The mutation prompt should be focused: only include todo tasks (not done/blocked), include the human's feedback, and instruct Claude to output a JSON array of task modifications. Format: [{"id": "FEAT-005", "action": "modify", "fields": {"notes": "updated notes", "acceptanceCriteria": [...]}}]. For new tasks, action="add" with full task object. For removal, action="irrelevant". Re-sync: read modified PRD JSON, call update_task for each changed task. The backup (.json.bak) is critical — if mutation corrupts the JSON, the user can recover.

correctness: Atomic write: temp file + rename, never partial writes
Backup before mutation: .json.bak preserves pre-mutation state
Re-sync must only update changed tasks, not re-import everything
Failure must not corrupt PRD JSON or DB state
performance: Use sonnet model (budget-conscious) for mutation calls
Only include todo tasks in mutation prompt (not done/blocked)
style: Follow update_prd_task_passes pattern for atomic JSON writes
Follow run_claude pattern for subprocess spawning
Mutation function belongs in prd_reconcile.rs alongside existing reconciliation code

userStories.title: Task mutation via Claude call after human review

userStories.description: apply_modifications_to_prd allows Claude to overwrite ANY field on story objects via `story[key] = value.clone()`, including passes, id, and requiresHuman. userStories.notes: Found during CODE-REVIEW-1. The DB sync_mutations_to_db already whitelists fields (title, description, notes, priority, acceptanceCriteria). Mirror this whitelist in apply_modifications_to_prd. The prompt instructs Claude to only modify certain fields, but prompt instructions aren't a reliable security boundary.

acceptanceCriteria: human_review_timeout i64-to-u32 conversion uses defensive clamping (negative values become None or 0)
All three sites updated: task.rs TryFrom<Row>, export/prd.rs load_tasks, engine.rs review query
Existing tests continue to pass
userStories.description: Multiple locations cast i64 to u32 for human_review_timeout via `v as u32`. A negative i64 would silently wrap to a large u32 (~136 years). Add defensive clamping: use `u32::try_from(v).ok()` or `v.max(0) as u32`.
userStories.notes: Found during CODE-REVIEW-1. Three sites: task.rs:385, export/prd.rs:203, engine.rs:1852. While negative values are unlikely from the DB, defensive coding prevents silent wrapping.
userStories.title: Defensive i64-to-u32 cast for human_review_timeout

userStories.description: handle_pause (signals.rs lines 84-94) has an inline stdin-reading loop nearly identical to the blocking branch of read_lines_with_timeout (lines 298-311). Refactor handle_pause to call read_lines_with_timeout(stdin.lock(), None) instead, eliminating the duplication and bringing handle_pause under 30 lines.
userStories.notes: Found during REFACTOR-REVIEW-1. The blocking branch of read_lines_with_timeout already handles the same pattern (read lines until empty/EOF). The only difference is handle_pause doesn't track saw_eof, but gaining the EOF warning is harmless. Make read_lines_with_timeout pub(crate) if needed.

userStories.description: Lines 1839-1889 of engine.rs embed ~50 lines of human review SQL query + loop + mutate_prd_from_feedback call inside the already-4000-line run_loop. Extract into a helper function (e.g., trigger_human_reviews) to reduce run_loop complexity.
userStories.notes: Found during REFACTOR-REVIEW-1. The block queries for requires_human tasks completed since completion_epoch_start, iterates them, calls handle_human_review, and optionally mutate_prd_from_feedback. This is a self-contained unit of work that can be cleanly extracted. Consider placing in prd_reconcile.rs or signals.rs depending on which module owns the concept.

default_registry() function returns registry pre-loaded with all 6 checks

userStories.description: Define SetupCheckRegistry as a collection of fn(&CheckContext) -> Vec<SetupCheck> function pointers. Implement default_registry() that registers all 6 existing checks. Replace run_all_setup_checks with registry iteration. Wire into audit_setup_with_claude_dir so the orchestrator never names individual checks — new checks are added by registering a function pointer.
userStories.notes: Keep it simple: type SetupCheckRegistry = Vec<fn(&CheckContext) -> Vec<SetupCheck>> or a newtype wrapper. The key win is that audit_setup_with_claude_dir no longer calls check functions by name — it iterates the registry. check_default_mode and similar single-result functions need a wrapper: |ctx| vec![check_default_mode(ctx)]. check_deny_conflicts and check_skills_installed already return Vec. Auto-fix logic remains separate — it keys off SetupCheck::name, not the registry.

correctness: Registry must produce identical results to direct calls in all cases
Ordering of checks in output must be deterministic
style: Prefer newtype over type alias for SetupCheckRegistry
default_registry() is the single source of truth for which checks run

userStories.description: Extract an EnvGuard RAII wrapper for env var test cleanup. Currently 9 tests across prd_reconcile.rs (CLAUDE_BINARY_MUTEX, 3 tests) and engine.rs (HINT_ENV_MUTEX, 6 tests) manually call set_var/remove_var. If a test panics before remove_var, the env var leaks to other tests. An EnvGuard that restores/removes the var on Drop fixes this.
userStories.notes: Pattern: let _guard = EnvGuard::set("KEY", "value"); — Drop restores previous value or removes var. The existing mutex guards should remain for serialization; EnvGuard handles cleanup only.
userStories.title: Extract EnvGuard RAII wrapper for panic-safe env var test cleanup

userStories.description: Two smaller DRY violations: (1) v15.rs has 4 near-identical pragma_table_info column-existence queries — extract column_exists(conn, table, column) -> bool helper. (2) engine.rs has 4 prefix-scoped query tests with identical boilerplate: prefix_and() → format SQL → build params Vec → execute — extract a helper that takes SQL template, prefix, and returns the query result.
userStories.notes: column_exists can be local to v15.rs test module (only used there). The prefix query helper could be local to engine.rs tests or in test_utils if useful elsewhere.
userStories.title: Extract column_exists and prefix query test helpers

userStories.description: Verify end-to-end flow: init a PRD with requiresHuman task, run loop (or simulate), verify pause triggers, verify guidance injected, verify mutation applied. Also verify cargo test + cargo clippy + cargo fmt --check all pass.
userStories.notes: Unit tests (TEST-001/002) already cover trigger_human_reviews query logic, mutation prompt construction, atomic writes, and graceful degradation. INT-001 should focus on CLI-level round-trip: (1) create a test PRD JSON with requiresHuman:true task, (2) task-mgr init imports it, (3) task-mgr show displays 'Requires Human Review: Yes', (4) task-mgr next includes requires_human in JSON, (5) task-mgr export round-trips both fields. The actual loop pause behavior requires manual testing or a scripted stdin pipe. Use test_utils::setup_test_db and insert_task_full for DB setup where needed.
